title: Woven tea towel
subtitle: A plain-weave linen-cotton tea towel woven on a rigid heddle or four-shaft loom
excerpt: Weave a tea towel approximately 45 x 60 cm in a plain weave with cotton or linen warp and a linen-cotton weft blend. The piece uses a striped warp for visual interest. Linen weft becomes more absorbent with every wash, so the finished towel improves over time.
sourceNotes: Woven tea towels are one of the oldest practical weaving projects and have been produced on home looms continuously since the 18th century. Method follows standard cotton/linen plain weave practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Linen fibre gains absorbency with repeated washing and use: the pectin coating on the raw fibre washes away progressively, exposing the hollow capillary structure of the flax cell. A new linen tea towel is less absorbent than an older one that has been washed dozens of times.

content.text: gets better with every wash. A towel woven this week will be a noticeably more useful cloth in five years. Cotton warp with linen weft is the best combination: cotton is strong under loom tension, and linen gives the absorbent working surface the towel needs.

items.name: 8/2 cotton warp yarn in two alternating stripe colours
Washed linen weft yarn (or linen-cotton blend)
Rigid heddle loom, at least 50 cm weaving width

content.text: A simple warp stripe is the easiest design element for a tea towel. Wind the warp alternating between two colours every 10 ends (approximately 7 cm at 15 dent). This produces 6-7 stripes across the 45 cm towel width. The weft shows as the overall cloth colour between the stripes; choosing a weft that contrasts with the warp gives the towel more visual depth than a matching weft.
Step 1: Warp and thread the loom

Measure the warp to 1.5 m (allowing for 40-50% warp takeup and shrinkage after wet-finishing). Thread 67 ends (for 45 cm at 15 dent) through the heddle in the alternating stripe pattern. Tie onto the front apron rod in groups and tension evenly.

Weave a 4 cm header of waste cotton to spread the warp. Weave the towel body in plain weave with the linen weft, using a firm, even beat. Linen is stiffer than cotton and requires slightly more pressure on the beater to set each pick. Weave approximately 85 cm on the loom to allow for the 30-40% weft shrinkage that happens when the linen washes.

Hemstitch both ends before cutting off. Machine-wash on a 60-degree cotton cycle (linen tolerates high temperatures). Press with a hot iron while damp. Fold and turn a 2 cm hem at each end and stitch by machine for a finished edge. After washing, the linen weft will have bloomed and the towel will have the slightly stiff, textured quality of new linen cloth; it softens with further washing and use.

items.problem: The towel is stiff and does not absorb after the first wash
items.cause: This is normal for a new linen towel; the stiffness reduces and absorbency increases with successive washing
items.fix: Wash the towel 5-6 times before assessing its final quality. Do not judge a new linen towel on its first wash performance.

items.problem: The towel has loose weft loops at the selvedge
items.cause: Linen is stiffer than wool and needs careful weft angle control at the selvedge
items.fix: Throw the weft in a high arc and pull back gently at the entry selvedge before beating. The arc compensates for linen's stiffness.
